title: Working with Skills
summary: Practical guide to using, installing, creating, and managing Hermes skills — on-demand knowledge documents that teach Hermes how to handle specific tasks — covering progressive disclosure loading, the Skills Hub, plugin-provided skills, and the skills-vs-memory decision.

cli_commands.purpose: Install a skill from a URL
Add a custom skill tap from a GitHub repo

config_keys.description: Per-skill configuration values prompted on first load

slash_commands.purpose: Install a skill from hub or URL
Load and activate a skill by its name

code_examples.caption: SKILL.md frontmatter
code_examples.code:
---
name: my-skill
description: Brief description of what this skill does
version: 1.0.0
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [my-tag, automation]
    category: my-category
---

# My Skill

## When to Use
Use this skill when the user asks about [specific topic].

## Procedure
1. First, check if [prerequisite] is available
2. Run `command --with-flags`

## Pitfalls
- Common failure: [description]. Fix: [solution]

code_examples.caption: Create and test a custom skill
code_examples.code:
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/skills/my-category/my-skill
# create SKILL.md
hermes chat -q "/my-skill help me with the thing"

gotchas: Installed skills take effect in new sessions — use /reset or --now flag for immediate availability in current session
Plugin skills are NOT listed in the system prompt skills index — they must be loaded explicitly by qualified name
Skills are zero tokens until loaded — they use progressive disclosure (skills_list → skill_view)
Memory is for facts (what things are); skills are for procedures (how to do things) — use the right one
The agent can create and update skills using skill_manage — let it save complex workflows for reuse
full_text:
# Working with Skills

Skills are on-demand knowledge documents that teach Hermes how to handle specific tasks. This guide walks you through using them day to day.
